name: B.A. in Law, Technology and Culture

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan-of-study terms are parsed (requirementGroups=0) even though this is a full B.A., so progression cannot be judged from a term sequence. The visible course cluster is, however, thematically tight and well-chosen for the major: a law-and-society history core (HIST 342 Civil Rights, 361 Founding, 362 Sex/Gender/Law, 364 American Law in the World, 369 Law and Society), a legal-reasoning skills course (STS 300 Legal Reasoning, Writing, and Technology), a research-methods course (HIST 395), and a doctrinal anchor (MGMT 290 Business Law I).
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the actual plan of study (the sibling Patent Law concentration has one) so the term-over-term progression from foundations to capstone is visible, not just a thematic course list.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: missingCourseCount=0 (no dangling references) and a shallow deepestPrereqChain=4 is appropriate for a humanities/law major; the law-and-society courses share a clean, uniform prerequisite (ENGL 102 plus a 200-level History/Humanities GER), which is coherent and accessible.
dimensions.suggestion: When the full plan is published, ensure the capstone (HSS 404/HIST 490) sits behind the STS 300 and HIST 395 skills courses so the light chain still scaffolds the research sequence.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only a course cluster with descriptions is provided. For an interdisciplinary law/technology/culture degree, explicit outcomes are important to show how the three strands integrate.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes describing what a graduate can do across law, technology, and culture (legal reasoning and writing, analysis of technology's social/legal impact, and research).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=120 is asserted, but with no parsed terms (summedTermCredits=null, creditReconcileDelta=null) the distribution cannot be verified and no gen-ed/core/elective split is visible. For a degree page this is a genuine gap, not merely a minor-style data caveat.
dimensions.suggestion: Render the full term-by-term plan so the 120 credits reconcile and the balance across gen-ed, legal/STS core, technology, and electives is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: For a full B.A., the page shows only an eight-course list with no plan of study, no term labels, and no credit accounting, so it reads as incomplete next to peer degree pages (including its own Patent Law concentration).
dimensions.suggestion: Add the complete plan-of-study rendering (terms, credits, required vs. elective) so the degree is presented as thoroughly as the concentration variant.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Conceptually the major is well-aimed at law-and-technology careers, and STS 300 Legal Reasoning, Writing, and Technology plus MGMT 290 Business Law give real professional grounding. But the eight visible courses are history/law heavy and the 'Technology' strand is under-represented -- privacy, cyberlaw, IP, and AI-governance courses (e.g., IT 331 Privacy and IT, IT 400 IT and the Law) that exist in the catalog do not surface here.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface concrete technology-law courses (privacy/IT 331, IT and the Law/IT 400, IP) in the core so the 'Technology' strand is as visible as the 'Law' and 'Culture' strands.

descriptionQuality.justification: The visible course descriptions are substantive and specific (HIST 364 American Law in the World, HIST 369 Law and Society, STS 300 Legal Reasoning), clearly conveying each course's scope.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are unambiguous and uniform (ENGL 102 plus a 200-level GER) with grade conditions, and missingCourseCount=0 means there are no dangling references in the cluster.

missingTopics.rationale: The base major shows MGMT 290 Business Law but no IP survey; HIST 384 Invention and Regulation exists in the catalog and would strengthen the technology-law core.

Catalog courses IT 331 Privacy and Information Technology and IT 400 Information Technology and the Law are not surfaced in this major, leaving data/cyber law -- central to 'law and technology' -- underweighted.

missingTopics.topic: AI and algorithmic law and ethics
missingTopics.rationale: There is no course on AI governance, automated decision-making, or algorithmic accountability, now a defining area of technology law.
STS 300 covers legal reasoning, but a broader technology-policy/regulation course is missing for a degree explicitly about law and technology.
HIST 395 Research Methods provides qualitative grounding, but there is no data/empirical-legal-analysis course connecting the major to legal analytics.

emergingTrends.rationale: A 'Law, Technology and Culture' degree is ideally positioned to own AI-governance content as regulation (e.g., the EU AI Act) matures.
GDPR/CCPA-style regimes and platform governance make privacy law a high-demand strand; integrating IT 331 would capture it.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital IP and generative-AI copyright/patent questions
emergingTrends.rationale: Generative AI is reshaping copyright and patent doctrine, a natural fit for the major's technology-law core.
Rising cyber-incident regulation and e-discovery create demand the major could serve by adding cyberlaw content.
Legal-tech and empirical legal studies are growing; a data-oriented methods option would modernize HIST 395's research training.

topSuggestions: Publish the full plan of study (terms, credits, required vs. elective) so the degree reconciles to its stated 120 credits and reads as completely as the Patent Law concentration.
Strengthen the 'Technology' strand by surfacing concrete technology-law courses (IP/HIST 384, privacy/IT 331, IT and the Law/IT 400) in the core.
Add AI-governance/algorithmic-accountability content to position the degree for the defining area of contemporary technology law.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes showing how law, technology, and culture integrate.
Add an empirical/computational legal-studies option to connect the major to legal analytics.
